widgets_values:
### 🧠 Impact Pack: The Detailer Logic

| Parameter | What it actually does | Impact on Speed | Impact on Quality |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Guide Size** | The **target resolution** for the internal crop. If a face is smaller than this, the node upscales the patch to this size before detailing. | 🔴 **HIGH.** Doubling this can quadruple render time (quadratic scaling). | 🟢 **SWEET SPOT.** Setting this to the model's native res (1024) gives the most "intelligent" results. |
| **Max Size** | The **hard limit**. If the crop (bbox * crop_factor) exceeds this, it is forced to downscale. This is your "Safety Valve." | 🟡 **MEDIUM.** Prevents your GPU from crashing on accidentally massive crops. | 🟡 **NEUTRAL.** Only matters if your crop factor is so large it exceeds your GPU's VRAM limit. |

---

### 🚀 The "Anima/Illustrious" Sweet Spot
Since you are using 1024-native models, use these settings to drop from 40s/step to ~2s/step:

* **guide_size: 1024** (Matches the model's training; prevents "over-detailing" or grainy skin).
* **max_size: 1280** (Gives a little breathing room for hair/necks without exploding the resolution).
* **guide_size_for: bbox** (Ensures the 1024 target applies specifically to the face, not the whole background crop).

### ⚠️ Why your high settings were "Bad" for Quality:
If your target is 2432px, the model sees a single skin pore as a giant crater. 
It tries to fill that "crater" with noise, resulting in:
1. **The "Sandpaper" Effect:** Excessive grain and micro-contrast.
2. **Double Features:** Two pupils or extra eyelashes (the model gets "lost" on such a huge canvas).
3. **Flatness:** Paradoxically, too many pixels can make the lighting look "averaged out" and flat.

nodes.title: Color Corrector
widgets_values: # You don't have to leave this on Auto mode. I am just lazy and it works good enough for me. It has manual settings and some other presets built in.

### 🧩 MASK to SEGS (Impact Pack)
Use this node to convert masks (from Segment Anything, manual painting, etc.) into "SEGS" for the Detailer.

* **The 'Combined' Setting:**
    * **TRUE (Default):** Merges all mask islands into one single bounding box. Fast, but less precise for scattered objects.
    * **FALSE:** Generates a separate "SEGS" entry for every disconnected part of the mask. Highly recommended for multiple subjects or distant objects.

* **The 'Drop Size' Parameter (Efficiency Booster):**
    * **What it does:** Sets a minimum pixel threshold. Any mask segment smaller than this number is ignored and "dropped" from the detailer pass.
    * **Why it matters:** If your mask has tiny accidental specks of "dust" or very small distant background objects, the Detailer will try to run a full sampling pass on each one. 
    * **Recommended Value:** **10 to 15**. This ensures only meaningful shapes are processed, saving you from wasting time on invisible artifacts.

* **The 'BBox Fill' Setting:**
    * **Status:** Usually keep this **FALSE**. If set to True, it ignores the specific shape of your mask and just details the entire square box around it, which can lead to "square-shaped" artifacts in your final image.

### 🛠️ The 'Force Inpaint' Toggle (Impact Pack)

One of the most common reasons a Detailer "does nothing" (even when it detects a face) is the interaction between `guide_size` and `force_inpaint`.

* **The Logic (force_inpaint: FALSE):**
    * By default, the Detailer is "lazy" to save you time. 
    * If the face it finds is **already larger** than your `guide_size` (e.g., your guide is 1024 but the face in the image is 1200px), the Detailer assumes the face already has enough resolution and **skips it entirely**.
    * This is why people often see the detector "box" appear, but no actual detail changes—the image was already "good enough" according to the settings.

* **The Logic (force_inpaint: TRUE):**
    * Setting this to **TRUE** disables the "Skip Logic."
    * The Detailer will run a full inpainting pass on the face **no matter how large it is**. Even if the face is 4K, it will crop it, process it at your guide_size, and stitch it back.

### ❓ Why isn't my Detailer doing anything?
If your Detailer is running but the image isn't changing, check these three things in order:
1. **Force Inpaint:** Set this to **TRUE** if you want to ensure the face is always refined.
2. **Denoise:** If your denoise is too low (e.g., 0.1), the changes might be too subtle to see.
3. **Drop Size:** If the face is very small and your `drop_size` is too high, the node will skip the face because it thinks it's just "noise."

nodes.title: Power Lora Loader
widgets_values:
# 🧠 Power Lora Loader (rgthree)

The **Power Lora Loader** is an advanced node from **rgthree’s ComfyUI extensions** that improves on the default Lora loader with extra tools for browsing, previewing, and managing Loras. It’s particularly useful if you work with models downloaded from **Civitai** or keep a large collection of Loras with metadata.

---

## ⚙️ Basic Functionality
- **Loads LoRAs** (Low-Rank Adapters) into a model pipeline, just like the normal `LoraLoader` node.
- Lets you **stack multiple LoRAs**, reorder them, and control their individual strengths.
- Supports **drag-and-drop loading** from the ComfyUI file browser.

---

## 🖱️ Right-Click Menu Features
Right-click on any loaded LoRA inside the node to open its context menu.

### 🔍 “Show Info”
- **Fetch info from civitai** from the LoRA’s embedded info or directly from its `model.json` or `civitai.info` file (if available).
- Shows:
  - Model Name
  - Version
  - Author
  - Trained Base Model
  - Civitai URL
  - Description (short)
  - Suggested Trigger Words
- If the `.safetensors` file has embedded metadata (Civitai automatically includes it), Power Lora Loader reads it locally — **no internet needed**.

---

## 🏷️ Trigger Word Tools

### 🧩 “Trained Words”
If the lora has any trigger words/trained words. They will be listed after fetching the info from civitai.
- There will be a listing of all known **trigger phrases** or **keywords** associated with that LoRA.
- You can **click on a trigger** to copy it directly to your clipboard.
- Useful for pasting into your prompt, prompt node, or **Markdown Note node**.
  
> 💡 You can also **Ctrl+Click** or **Shift+Click** multiple trigger words to copy them together, separated by commas.

---

## 🖼️ Image Previews
After fetching the info from civitai, it will also display sample images toward the bottom of the popup window.

If the LoRA has **multiple preview images**, you can cycle through them (small arrows appear when more than one image is found).

---

## 📦 Multi-LoRA Controls
- Add multiple LoRAs to the same node with the ➕ button.
- Adjust **strength (weight)** sliders per LoRA.
- Reorder LoRAs with drag handles.
- Toggle individual LoRAs on/off with checkboxes.

---

# 🎨 VAE Source Control
---

### ⚠️ Issue: "Washed Out" or Gray Images?
If your generations look desaturated or "foggy," your checkpoint likely lacks a **Baked-in VAE**.

#### **How to Fix:**
1. **Load a VAE:** Ensure a valid VAE (like *vae-ft-mse-840000-ema*) is selected in the **Load VAE** node above.
2. **Toggle the Switch:** Set the VAE Source toggle to **`False`**.

---
* **TRUE:** Uses the VAE included inside your Checkpoint model.
* **FALSE:** Uses the external VAE selected in the Load VAE node.

## 🧩 Missing Node: Lora Prompt Concatenation
---

### ⚠️ Issue: "Custom node not found"
If your workflow loads with a red "missing node" error for **Lora Prompt Concatenation**, you need to install the **GPS Supplements** pack.

#### **Method 1: ComfyUI Manager (Easiest)**
1. Open **ComfyUI Manager** > **Custom Nodes Manager**.
2. Type **`GPS`** in the search bar.
3. Find **"GPS' Supplements for ComfyUI"** and click **Install**.
4. Restart ComfyUI.

#### **Method 2: Manual Installation**
If you prefer to install via Git or a browser:
* **GitHub Repository:** [comfyui-gps-supplements](https://github.com/Goshe-nite/comfyui-gps-supplements)
* **URL:** `https://github.com/Goshe-nite/comfyui-gps-supplements`

---
> **Note:** This node is essential for the "Not So Simple" logic as it handles sending the lora model info to Image Saver for detection when uploading to CivitAI.

# 👿 Detail Daemon: Advanced Tuning
---

### ⚠️ The Golden Rule
* **`detail_amount`:** Keep this under **0.45**. 
* Values higher than 0.45 often introduce "deep fried" artifacts or grit that ruins the 2.5D aesthetic. Use with caution!

---

### ⚙️ Precision Settings (The Gaps)

* **Start / End:** Usually set to `0.0` and `1.0`. Adjusting **End** to `0.8` can prevent the Daemon from messing with the final "smooth" pixels of the generation.
* **Bias & Exponent:** 
    * **Bias:** Controls which parts of the image get detail. Positive values favor lighter areas; negative favors shadows.
    * **Exponent:** Adjusts the "contrast" of the detail. High values (2.0+) make the detail more aggressive/sharp.
* **Offsets (Start/End):** These create a "safety buffer." A **Start_Offset** of `0.1` ensures the initial composition is locked in before the Daemon starts adding texture.
* **Fade:** The "Strength" of the transition. Higher values smooth out the injection of detail so it doesn't look like an "overlay."
* **Smooth (Toggle):** Set to **TRUE** for your semi-realistic anime style. It prevents the detail from becoming too "noisy" or grainy.
* **CFG Scale Override:** Set to `0` (Off) by default. If your image looks washed out only when using the Daemon, set this to match your main Sampler CFG.

---

> **Strategy:** Use this at low amounts (0.15 - 0.25) to add skin/fabric texture. Let the **SMC-CFG** handle the big structures, and let the **Daemon** handle the "micro-pores."

nodes.title: Sigmas Rescale
widgets_values:
### **Sigmas Rescale (RES4LYF) Recommended Settings**

The **Sigmas Rescale** node adjusts the noise intensity (Sigma Schedule) rather than just slicing steps. This provides better control over contrast and detail compared to standard denoise.

#### **1. High-Contrast / Heavy Texture**
*Best for: Deep shadows, high-fidelity materials (satin/leather), and sharp contrast.*
* **Start:** `16.0`
* **End:** `0.02`
* *Note: High start values act like a "contrast booster." The 0.02 end value prevents over-sharpening artifacts.*

#### **2. Balanced / Semi-Realistic**
*Best for: Clean 2.5D aesthetics and realistic skin textures.*
* **Start:** `9.0`
* **End:** `0.00`
* *Note: Provides a natural balance of light and shadow without the "crunchy" look of higher values.*

#### **3. Soft / Low Contrast**
*Best for: Softer lighting and smoother gradients.*
* **Start:** `6.0` to `7.5`
* **End:** `0.00`
* *Note: This is the minimum threshold for a usable image. Values below 6.0 often appear washed out or white.*

---
**Technical Tip:**
Unlike standard KSampler denoise, a **Start** of `1.0` is too low for a full generation and will result in a blown-out white image. Start high and work down to find the desired intensity.

# ⚠️ Global Control Center
---

### 🛡️ Master Override Logic
* **Global Seed:** This node acts as the single source of truth, **overriding the seed value on ALL nodes** across the entire workflow.
* **Global Sampler:** This will force its sampler and scheduler settings onto **every sampler node** in the chain. 

> **Important:** Ensure your chosen sampler/scheduler combo is compatible with all active nodes to prevent "Validation Error" crashes.

---

### 🧪 RES4LYF & Beta57 Users
If you intend to use the **beta57** scheduler (via RES4LYF):
1. **Bypass** this Global Sampler node.
2. Manually select **beta57** within the *Sampler Scheduler Settings* nodes inside the **Initial Image** and **USDU** groups.
3. *Note:* Specialized nodes (like those in the **Impact Pack**) do not support this custom scheduler and may error if forced.

---

### 🔓 Manual Mode
**Bypass these Global groups** if you prefer to define seeds, samplers, or schedulers individually at the local node level.

nodes.title: Epsilon Scaling
widgets_values:
#### This node implements the Epsilon Scaling method from the research paper "Elucidating the Exposure Bias in Diffusion Models" (arxiv.org/abs/2308.15321v6). It works by scaling the predicted noise during the sampling process to help reduce exposure bias, which can lead to improved quality in the generated images. This implementation uses the "uniform schedule" recommended by the paper for its practicality and effectiveness.

### Inputs

| Parameter | Data Type | Required | Range | Description |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `model` | MODEL | Yes | - | The model to which the epsilon scaling patch will be applied. |
| `scaling_factor` | FLOAT | No | 0.5 - 1.5 | The factor by which the predicted noise is scaled. A value greater than 1.0 reduces the noise, while a value less than 1.0 increases it (default: 1.005). |

nodes.title: CFG Zero Star
widgets_values: #### The CFGZeroStar node applies a specialized guidance scaling technique to diffusion models. It modifies the classifier-free guidance process by calculating an optimized scale factor based on the difference between conditional and unconditional predictions. This approach adjusts the final output to provide enhanced control over the generation process while maintaining model stability.

# ⚠️ Wildcard Stability Guide
---

### 🎲 Why is my prompt still changing?
Setting the **Global Seed** to "Fixed" only locks the sampler noise; it **does not** stop the Wildcard node from repopulating new strings. To lock in a specific wildcard result, you must interact directly with the **ImpactWildcardProcessor**.

### 🛠️ How to Lock your Prompt
1. **Set Mode to `reproduce`:** This is the only way to freeze the current wildcard selection.
2. **The "Auto-Reset" Trap:** Be aware that this node may automatically switch back to `populate` after a retry or a fresh generation. 
3. **User Input vs. Output:** Setting the seed to "Fixed" only stabilizes your *input* text—it does **not** stabilize the final *output* string generated by the wildcard logic.

---

> **Pro-Tip:** If you've found a "Golden Prompt" via wildcards, copy the text from the lower box of the Wildcard node and paste it into the positive prompt instead to avoid the hassle of the wildcard switching back to 'populate' and then bypass the Wildcards group.

nodes.title: Laplace Scheduler
widgets_values:
### **Laplace Scheduler Configuration Guide**

The Laplace Scheduler uses a probability distribution to manage noise levels (sigmas) throughout the sampling process. Adjusting these values allows for granular control over the "texture" and "weight" of the final image.

---

#### **1. Mu (μ) — The Distribution Center**
This setting determines where the density of sampling steps is focused.
* **Default (0.0):** Standard balanced distribution.
* **Negative Values (e.g., -0.6):** Shifts sampling density toward the mid-to-late steps. 
    * **Effect:** Increases micro-details, deepens shadows, and enhances material textures (leather, satin, skin).
* **Positive Values:** Shifts focus to the early structural steps.
    * **Effect:** Results in cleaner, flatter, and more traditional anime-style outputs.

---

#### **2. Beta (β) — The Scale/Spread**
This controls how "wide" the noise distribution spreads across the steps.
* **Default (0.5):** A standard, smooth curve.
* **Higher Values (e.g., 0.8):** Widens the distribution (fatter tails).
    * **Effect:** Introduces more "grit" and complexity. It prevents the model from over-smoothing the image, helping to avoid a "plastic" or "shiny" look.
* **Lower Values:** Tightens the distribution around the Mu point.
    * **Effect:** Creates smoother gradients and simpler color fields.

---

#### **Quick Reference Table**

| Style | Mu (μ) | Beta (β) | Visual Result |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Standard** | 0.0 | 0.5 | Balanced, clean anime look. |
| **Textured/Dark** | -0.6 | 0.8 | Detailed, high-contrast, "2.5D" feel. |
| **Soft/Clean** | 0.2 | 0.4 | Bright, soft transitions, less grit. |

---

> **Note:** If images appear too "crunchy" or overly dark, move **Mu** closer to 0 or slightly reduce **Beta**.
---
### **Initial Image Step Guide (Laplace)**

Since this workflow uses the **Laplace Scheduler** for the base generation, step count behaves differently than standard schedulers.

* **Recommended: 28 – 32 Steps**
    * **28 Steps:** Best for most "2.5D" anime styles. Provides clean textures and solid anatomy.
    * **32 Steps:** Use this if the initial image feels too "flat" or if you want deeper, more dramatic shadows before the upscale.
* **Why not 35+?**
    * On the base pass, excessive steps can "over-bake" the image, making it too dark or "crunchy." This can cause the Upscaler (USDU) to create artifacts or "grainy" skin textures.

---
**Tip:** If the base image looks too "busy" or noisy, try lowering the steps to 25. If it looks too "plastic," increase toward 30.

nodes.title: Use Denoised Output?
widgets_values:
## 🛁 Output Fidelity: Clean vs. Latent Noise
---

### 🧪 What is the difference?
This toggle determines how the image is passed from the initial sampler to the next stage (VAE Decode or Upscale).

* **Denoised Output (TRUE):**
    * **The "Clean" Path:** It mathematically removes 100% of the remaining noise from the latent.
    * **Pros:** Best for final renders or when you want maximum clarity.
    * **Cons:** Can sometimes introduce "ringing" artifacts or a "plastic" look if the model over-denoises.

* **Normal Output (FALSE):**
    * **The "Detail" Path:** It leaves a microscopic "grain" of latent noise on the image.
    * **Pros:** This noise acts as "seeds" for the Upscale/USDU pass to grow extra fine details (pores, fabric weave, etc.).
    * **Cons:** If your Upscale Denoise is too high, this leftover noise is exactly what causes **Hallucinations**.

nodes.title: USDU Hallucinations
widgets_values:
## 🌀 Preventing USDU Hallucinations (by Gemini)

If your upscale is "hallucinating" extra limbs, faces, or objects (like extra couches/sleeves), use these techniques to keep the tiles coherent:

#### 🔹 1. The "Denoise Ceiling"
* **Recommended:** `0.30` to `0.35` (Max)
* **The Rule:** Anything above 0.40 gives the model too much freedom to "invent" new subjects inside a tile. If you still see hallucinations at 0.35, drop to **0.20 - 0.28**.

#### 🔹 2. Tile Padding (The "Context" Fix)
* **Recommended:** `128` to `512`
* **Why it works:** Padding allows each tile to "see" a bit of the surrounding area. Without enough padding, a tile containing a sleeve doesn't know there is an arm inside it, so it might turn the fabric into a new face.

#### 🔹 3. Seam Fix Mode
* **Recommended:** `None` or `Band Pass`
* **Note:** Using "Half Tile" or "Area" seam fixes can sometimes double the chances of hallucinations because they create a second pass over the intersections. If your seams look fine, leave this off.
#### 🔍 What is "Band Pass" Seam Fix?
* **Recommendation:** Use this if you see visible "grid lines" in your final upscale.
* **How it works:** It mathematically blends the color and lighting at the tile edges without "re-drawing" the area. 
* **Benefit:** It is much less likely to cause hallucinations than "Half Tile" or "Area" modes because it doesn't give the model a second chance to invent new objects at the seams.

# 👁️ Detailer & Detection Troubleshooting
---

### 🛠️ Issue: "The Detailer is ruining my image!"
If you are seeing weird artifacts—like only one eye being fixed while the other is ignored—follow this stability checklist:

1. **Lock the Seed:** Change your **Global Seed** mode to **`fixed`**. 
2. **Handle Wildcards:** If using wildcards, set the **ImpactWildcardProcessor** to **`reproduce`**.
   * *Note:* If you retry, you **must** ensure it hasn't reset to `populate`.
3. **Force Focus:** Type `eye` (or the specific part you are fixing) into the **wildcard_spec** field on the Detailer node to guide the model.
4. **The ComfyUI "Glitch":** Recent updates sometimes force a full restart even on a fixed seed. If it still rerolls, try bypass/unbypass the node to "nudge" it.

---

### 🎯 Issue: "The Detector is flagging the wrong things!"
*(Example: Face detection picking up anatomy it shouldn't)*

* **Increase Threshold:** Raise the **threshold** on the **BBOX Detector SEGS** node. This makes the model "stricter" about what it calls a face.
* **Model Limits:** Some detection models have "false positives" baked into their training. If it keeps failing, try swapping to a different detector model.

---

### 🔍 Issue: "The Detector isn't finding anything!"

* **Decrease Threshold:** Lower the **threshold** value. This makes the detector more "desperate" to find a match.
* **The Last Resort:** If the model still won't see it, the subject might be too small or too obscured in the latent space for that specific detection model to register.

nodes.title: If you are going to use the 2nd USDU
widgets_values:
# 2nd USDU Recommended Settings
---
### I recommend using lower denoise and higher steps in comparison to your first USDU settings.

### You may need to adjust CFG up/down.
--- 
### The 2nd USDU pass can often ruin textures or give muddy results depending on your settings. In my experience, it is not good for stuff like embroidery or lace. YMMV.

### The tile sizes are tied to the width and height output from the *Get Image Size & Count* node to the left of the *USDU 1* group. Just disconnect the tile width and height inputs on the 2nd USDU node if you want to set these manually.

widgets_values:
